reviews.review_text: The downfall of the Payday franchise should be studied. I've sunk so much time and money into this game because I genuinely used to love it (yes even the broken drills) but between bad business practices, dlc (in 2026 AFTER PAYDAY 3), the infamous subscription and Payday 3. I can't support this anymore. It's not jumping on the hate train, it's watching something I love die.

Left 4 Dead but you rob people. + Stealth Play as well that's both challenging and fun. Great progression, not pay-to-win ftmp. Some DLC should be purchased but you can see what things are part of what DLC and each one is like $3. Great gameplay loop, and OFFLINE PLAY. Isn't THAT exciting in 2026? It will feel early on like your typical online game when you see continental coins, trust, these are NOT microtransactional, they exponentially become more available as you progress. The grind is deeply tied to the real, human pace of the game. Yes, you'll revisit or grind a few missions, but as you do, it's less "Oh let's get my numbers up" and more "I'm learning so much about this game, I'm going to be so much better at this next time." I slept on this one for years. Exceptional. You GOT to pay attention to the map sidebar. The tutorials, and the campaign are in there, as well as new modes you unlock once you've gotten competent enough. Career/Campaign really just ties the experience together for offline play and it's great to get slightly more challenge as you go. When you become able to finally fight in Mayhem difficulty, you will truly get that rush of a proper progression system.

Love being a alcoholic carrying a minigun. 
Great game 10/10 sooo much to do.

This game holds a special place in my heart. During the COVID lockdown I used to grind this game for countless hours. It may not be the most realistic and also many of the heists dont make any sense but it is still a masterpiece.

This game was great, but I don't have a second mortgage to buy everything. This game costs like 10 bucks for all dlcs is ~200, that is a 2000% increase. Jesus Christ Overkill, the full price of your game with dlcs should not exceed its original price, if you need a subscription to trade off the costs of your dlcs then you have an issue. Also the devs have like the most brain dead ideas for licensing like they lose scarface license fine, but take out half of the dlc like the character perk deck and gun was so dumb and they left in the heist. All in all Payday 2 was one of the best games Ive played, but the devs are running it to the ground with their greed and impotence.

👍 Recommended
🕒 12.9 hours on record  

Payday 2 is still the best game in the Payday series and remains one of the most fun co-op heist games ever made. With 12.9 hours on record, I quickly remembered why this game has such a strong legacy even after all these years.

The gameplay loop is excellent — planning and executing heists, going loud or going stealth, and working together with your crew creates incredibly satisfying moments. The sheer amount of content is massive thanks to years of DLCs, which add tons of weapons, masks, maps, and heists. There’s always something new to try or grind for.

It’s especially great with friends. The chaos when a stealth run goes loud, the clutch moments, and the yelling over voice chat make it an absolute blast in co-op. Even today, Payday 2 feels better than Payday 3 for many players.

The only real downside is that the game can be quite buggy at times, and some mechanics feel dated. However, the fun factor easily outweighs those issues.

If you enjoy co-op heist games and don’t mind the occasional jank, Payday 2 is still highly recommended. A true classic that delivers when played with friends. 💰🔫👍
The replayablility of this game is unmatched! Awesome game, I wish they would update the graphics tho.
This game is amazing, worth every penny, timeless, endless and with friends tons of fun

this is a pretty good game (especially with friends, but you could still have fun without any friends). My one complaint is with DLC, because there is a TON of DLC, and you could either buy all of them over time or you could pay for a 7 dollar subscription to play all the DLC stuff.  However the DLC stuff is pretty good (Especially Big Bank and the Golden Grin).

An absolute classic that defined the cooperative heist genre. After all these years, it still remains the undisputed king of bank robbing games.

The build variety is insane, allowing you to go stealthy like a ghost or loud with a heavy LMG and body armor. The soundtrack by Simon Viklund is legendary and gets your adrenaline pumping the second the assault wave starts.

Even with its age and tons of DLC, the chaotic gameplay loop with friends never gets boring. Packing bags and drilling vaults is a lifestyle. 10/10

Ultimate Co-op Crime
Payday 2 is all about intense tactical planning, chaotic shootouts, and the ultimate power trip of a perfect heist. The loop of casing a bank, drilling vaults, and holding off endless waves of police to secure millions in loot is incredibly thrilling and addictive.

It’s simple to jump into, but mastering the intricate stealth mechanics and building the perfect character skill tree takes serious dedication. Watching a high-stakes, 30-minute plan come together perfectly with your squad never gets old.

While the massive amount of DLC walls, aging engine jank, and brutal difficulty spikes can frustrate solo players, the cooperative depth is legendary.

If you’re looking for a definitive, action-packed crime simulator with endless replayability and a killer soundtrack, this is a masterpiece. 9/10

8/10. If it's for you, you'll love it.
Game itself is great. Definitely worth at least checking out, especially at $10. Ran great on my PC, no game-breaking bugs, mechanics or errors. Lots of variety in how you approach missions to sink your teeth into. Incredibly unique concept that scratches an itch for me that no other game really satisfies. However, it's not perfect. The levels can become repetitive- bust in, grab loot, kill cops, and get out. Over, and over and over again. The game does a good job coming up with new and exciting things to do in each mission, different twists and turns to surprise the core loop, but at the end of the day, it's the same core concept from the first robbery to the white house mission. Loud or Silent. I don't know how I'd fix that honestly, but the developers sure tried their hardest and I applaud that. Some of my favorite videogame levels yet are in this game.
Secondly, the direction the developers have taken since this game came out, and especially since Payday 3 launched, have been depressing. Like, why on earth do I need a PAYDAY SUBSCRIPTION?? Why does the DLC cost like 5 times the actual game??  They're obviously milking this cow as long as it lasts.
Other things: 
-The Lore is there, you just have to remember what all is happening between the heists. This game never emphasizes the importance of knowing who its characters are beyond the name of your heister and your squadmates'. But it's there if you want to dig. The way the missions are played out on Crime.net (The online of offline missions modes that take you to the map screen) are out of story order anyways. Again, it's story is never treated as essential.
- Man, the cars suck in this game. Such as, the level where you shield a guy fixing a motorcycle is fine, another unique, well-executed level, but MAN driving the motorcycles at the end makes it my LEAST favorite mission. We spent an extra 10 minutes of a 25 min level just trying to get a dang vehicle through a drainage hole and ended up just jumping over the bike and walking to the end. 

TL;DR - Good game. If this seems like your kind of fun, have a go at it. If not, that's fine. Just don't buy the subscription.

If you have ever dreamed of pulling off the perfect heist, PAYDAY 2 is the game for you. Even after all these years, it remains the definitive cooperative shooter that perfectly balances high-octane action with strategic planning.

Why I highly recommend it:

Co-op Excellence: This game is built for teamwork. Coordinating with your crew to disable cameras, bypass security, and drill into vaults creates some of the most memorable gaming moments I’ve ever had.

Insane Variety: With hundreds of weapons, masks, skills, and heists ranging from simple jewelry store robberies to complex multi-day bank jobs, you will never run out of things to do.

The "One More Run" Factor: Whether you go in loud with heavy armor and LMGs or stick to stealth and precision, the satisfaction of completing a difficult mission on the highest difficulty is unmatched.

Incredible Soundtrack: Simon Viklund’s score is legendary. The music reacts to the intensity of the heist, making every shootout feel like a high-budget action movie.

PAYDAY 2 is an absolute blast, especially with friends. It’s loud, it’s stylish, and it’s undeniably fun. Put on your mask, grab your drill, and get to work!

Verdict: 10/10 – The gold standard of co-op shooters.

PAYDAY 2 (Overkill Software) is a co‑op heist shooter for up to 4 players. Pros: team‑based gameplay, tactical choices (stealth or firefight), deep progression system (15 skill trees), vast arsenal (over 200 weapons), high replayability, and atmospheric soundtrack.
Cons: outdated graphics, numerous DLCs, weak AI in solo mode, high difficulty at later levels, and an awkward Crime.net interface.
Overall, it’s an engaging game for fans of co‑op shooters — especially when playing with friends.

Payday 2 is the gift that keeps on giving.

For a game that by all rights should have been winding down years ago, and literally has multiple times, Payday 2 just refuses to die. It has had one of the [b]strangest most impressive lifespans of any co-op shooter[/b] I've ever played. New content, fixes, weapon packs, QOL, and community attention have kept it alive long after most games would have been left to rot by their developers. I believe that alone gives Payday 2 a reason to respect it.

That [b]certainly does not mean the ride has been perfectly smooth[/b]. My word, the game has had some ups and downs. Some of the worst balance decisions I've ever witnessed in a game, technical jank from an ancient racing game engine, genuine content bloat, occasionally having updates that make you question your purpose in life. Man. But all things considered this game has an incredibly strong identity. Even with mods replacing a heister with an anime girl, you can still see Paydays core clearly defined in its gameplay. With Sidetrack taking the reins on support now, it feels like Payday 2 is in good hands. There's a clear sense that the people working on it understand rather well what makes the game special.

I believe Payday's biggest strength is [b]co-op[/b]. I played alone, entirely offline, for years. After finally diving into the game with a friends I can easily say that is where Payday 2 becomes fully alive. Coordinating your heist plans, botching stealth, filing early retirement forms with the boys, and ripping your friend from deaths grip with inspire so you can get the last bags in the truck is genuinely a mythical experience. The chaos that comes with a crew is just something else man.

The art direction also deserves some credit. [b]Graphically this games not much special, but it has style[/b]. The masks, menus, weapons, enemy designs, and the heist locations as well, all give Payday 2 a very distinct personality. It has this grimy, over the top crime movie energy that makes it instantly recognizable. The engine is bad though, no two ways about it. 

One thing that cannot be glanced over is [b]Simon Viklund's music[/b]. Payday 2 would not be the same game without it. The way the music builds from stealthy tension to assault chaos is a huge part of what makes a heist feel fun. Razormind, The Mark, I Will Give You My All, Break The Rules, And Now We Run, and my personal favorite of Viklunds entire library, This Is Our Time. The backing to your heists matters more than you realize. Simon Viklund, as well as some other artists, even Alesso, have brought some of the best bangers my ears have ever been graced with, Chains is in a Pickle not withstanding. Every heist feels like a movie climax, every escape feels desperate, even when you're not stressing on revives. I could go on for hours about the music.

[b]Gunplay and movement[/b] are another huge part of why the game still works. Payday 2 is fast, aggressive, and arcadey in the best, most jank way. Weapons feel punchy and snappy, and builds are suprisingly in depth. This is something I've re-experienced recently while teaching a friend the game. The movement also gives you enough freedom to feel like you're constantly pushing foreward, even while you hiding behind cover. It's not a realistic shooter, and it has issues with terrain, hitboxes for objects, the lot. But it knows exactly what kind of power fantasy it's trying to be the overwhelming majority of the time, and the gunplay makes up for the shortcomings of Paydays movement jank. 

All things considered, this game is genuinely legendary.

The undisputed king of the co-op heist genre that refuses to be dethroned.

PAYDAY 2 is a loud, chaotic, and incredibly deep masterpiece that has aged like a fine wine—or perhaps a very expensive stolen bottle of scotch. While modern sequels like PAYDAY 3 have largely flopped due to technical issues, lack of content, and questionable design choices, the second game stands taller than ever. It is the definitive heist fantasy, offering a staggering amount of variety that the industry simply hasn't been able to match. Whether you’re trying to pull off a perfect, high-tension "Shadow Raid" stealth run without ever being seen, or you’re hunkered down behind a ballistic shield while "Razormind" blasts in your ears as you fight off an entire city's worth of police, the gameplay loop is pure adrenaline.

The sheer scale of the content available in the Definitive Edition is almost overwhelming in the best way possible. With over 70 heists, dozens of playable characters, and an arsenal of weapons and masks that would make a small country jealous, the customization is unparalleled. The perk deck and skill tree systems allow for incredibly specific builds; you can play as a dodge-based speedster, a tanky "Stoic" who shrugs off damage with a flask of whiskey, or a mastermind who convinces the police to fight for you. It’s a game that rewards mastery and coordination, turning a group of four friends into a well-oiled criminal machine.

Looking back, it’s clear that PAYDAY 2 captured lightning in a bottle that the franchise is currently struggling to find again. It has a soul, a grit, and a sense of community that makes it feel alive even a decade after its release. The "jank" that was once a criticism has become part of its charm—a reminder of an era where games were allowed to be a little messy as long as they were incredibly fun. If you’re looking for the ultimate co-op experience, skip the "modern" alternatives and come back to the classics. The masks are ready, the drill is jammed, and the thermal bore is waiting.

10/10 — Guys, the thermal drill, go get it. Still the best heist in town.
